tasks.title: Create shared types and handler base class
tasks.description: Create patchy_bot/types.py with a HandlerContext dataclass that wraps the shared state handlers need (cfg, store, qbt, plex, tvmeta, llm, rate_limiter, user_flow, user_nav_ui, progress_tasks, chat_history). Create patchy_bot/handlers/base.py with a BaseHandler ABC that accepts this context. This establishes the contract all extracted handlers will follow.
tasks.details: HandlerContext bundles all shared state into one object so extracted handler modules have clean, explicit dependencies instead of reaching back into BotApp. The BaseHandler ABC defines register_callbacks() and register_commands() methods that each handler implements. Create patchy_bot/handlers/__init__.py as well.
tasks.testStrategy: Import types.py and base.py, instantiate HandlerContext with dummy values, create a trivial BaseHandler subclass. Run existing test suite — must still pass with 162 tests.

tasks.title: Build the callback dispatcher
tasks.description: Create patchy_bot/dispatch.py with a CallbackDispatcher class supporting exact-match and prefix-match registration. Replace the on_callback 1,251-line if/elif chain in bot.py with a dispatcher that routes to the same existing methods (still on BotApp). This is a mechanical refactor — behavior does not change, only the dispatch mechanism.
tasks.details: The dispatcher supports two registration modes: register_exact(data, handler) for exact string matches like 'nav:home', and register_prefix(prefix, handler) for prefix matches like 'rm:'. Prefixes are sorted by length descending for longest-match-first. In this task, all 53 existing prefixes are registered on the dispatcher but still call the same BotApp methods. The on_callback method shrinks to: auth check, answer query, dispatch call.
tasks.testStrategy: Register all 53 current prefixes on the dispatcher. Unit test that each prefix routes to the correct method name. Run existing callback tests — all must pass. Deploy and verify all Telegram button flows work.

subtasks.title: Design CallbackDispatcher class with exact and prefix match
subtasks.description: Create patchy_bot/dispatch.py with register_exact(), register_prefix(), and dispatch() methods. Include type hints and docstrings.

subtasks.title: Register all 53 callback prefixes on the dispatcher
subtasks.description: Map every existing if/elif branch to a dispatcher registration. Group by domain: nav (1), add/download (3), remove (18), schedule (18), menu (9), flow (3), stop (1).

subtasks.title: Replace on_callback if/elif chain with dispatcher.dispatch() call
subtasks.description: Reduce on_callback to: auth check, q.answer(), cleanup ephemeral, dispatcher.dispatch(data, ...). Move the try/except error handling into the dispatcher.

subtasks.title: Add dispatcher unit tests and verify existing callback tests pass
subtasks.description: Write tests that register a few prefixes and verify dispatch routes correctly. Run full test suite to confirm zero regressions.

Move render helpers (_render_nav_ui, _render_flow_ui, _render_remove_ui, _render_schedule_ui, _render_tv_ui, ephemeral cleanup) into patchy_bot/ui/rendering.py. In bot.py, replace each moved method with a delegation call or direct import.

The keyboard methods include: _nav_footer, _home_only_keyboard, _compact_action_rows, _command_center_keyboard, _tv_filter_choice_keyboard, _media_picker_keyboard, _stop_download_keyboard, _remove_prompt_keyboard, _remove_browse_root_keyboard, _remove_candidate_keyboard, _remove_confirm_keyboard, _remove_show_action_keyboard, _remove_season_action_keyboard, _remove_paginated_keyboard, _schedule_candidate_keyboard, _schedule_preview_keyboard, _schedule_missing_keyboard, _schedule_episode_picker_keyboard, _schedule_picker_keyboard, _schedule_dl_confirm_keyboard. Create patchy_bot/ui/__init__.py.

The handler registers itself with the dispatcher for a:, d:, and p: callback prefixes. Update bot.py on_text movie/tv flow branches to delegate to the search handler.
tasks.details: The search handler manages the full lifecycle: query parsing with argparse, torrent search via QBClient, result filtering (quality tier, size, seeds), pagination, and the add-to-library flow. It handles 3 callback prefixes: a: (show library picker), d: (download with choice), p: (page navigation). The on_text branches for mode='movie'/stage='await_title' and mode='tv'/stage='await_title' delegate to this handler's run_search method. Lines moved: approximately 2791-3257 from bot.py (~465 lines).

tasks.title: Extract the schedule handler

tasks.details: The schedule system is the largest domain in bot.py. It includes: runner configuration methods (interval, grace, retry, backoff), source health tracking, bootstrap logic, TVMaze bundle/cache management, probe logic (filesystem inventory, Plex inventory, episode status), auto-download with episode ranking, notification system, 18 keyboard builders, 8 text builders, the background runner job (120s interval), and the download-episode flow. Depends on task 5 because schedule auto-download calls _do_add and progress tracking methods. The _backup_job (line 2305) runs as a daily scheduled job and calls store.backup().

tasks.details: This is the last major extraction. Command handlers are thin dispatchers — cmd_search sets up a flow and calls the search handler, cmd_schedule calls the schedule handler, etc. The main complexity is that many cmd_* methods reference other domain handlers. After tasks 4-7 extract those domains, the command handlers become simple delegation wrappers. Also includes: _health_report (~95 lines of system health checks), _speed_report (~50 lines), and the menu:* callback group which navigates between Command Center screens. The on_error method handles unhandled exceptions.

tasks.details: The LLM chat system is the smallest domain (~65 lines). It uses PatchyLLMClient to generate responses via an OpenAI-compatible API. The system prompt includes the bot's personality and optional qBT status snapshot. Chat history is maintained per-user in an LRU-bounded dict. Currently marked as a re-enablement target in CLAUDE.md. The _reply_patchy_chat method is the fallback handler in on_text when no other flow or intent matches.

tasks.title: Document qBittorrent search plugin optimization
tasks.description: Document the completed work that disabled duplicate qBittorrent search plugins (EZTV, YTS standalone) that overlapped with Jackett indexers, reducing enabled plugins from 9 to 7 via qBT API.
tasks.details:
This task documents work that was already completed outside of the tracked refactor tasks. The qBittorrent search system uses plugins that are enabled/disabled via the qBittorrent WebUI API v2 (/api/v2/search/plugins endpoint). The standalone EZTV and YTS plugins were disabled because they duplicate functionality already provided by Jackett indexers, which aggregate multiple sources more efficiently.

**What was done:**
1. Identified that EZTV and YTS standalone plugins overlapped with Jackett indexers
2. Used the qBittorrent WebUI API v2 to disable these plugins (likely via /api/v2/search/enablePlugin with enabled=false)
3. Reduced the total enabled plugin count from 9 to 7
4. Preserved the Jackett integration which provides broader coverage

**Current state:**
- QBClient.list_search_plugins() in patchy_bot/clients/qbittorrent.py:250-252 reads plugin state via GET /api/v2/search/plugins
- All searches use plugin="enabled" by default (see handlers/search.py:43, handlers/schedule.py:1123, bot.py:2433, clients/qbittorrent.py:63)
- The /health command reports enabled plugin count via commands.py:217-218
- No programmatic plugin enable/disable methods exist in QBClient — changes were made manually via qBT WebUI or direct API calls

**Documentation needs:**
1. Add a comment in QBClient or CLAUDE.md explaining which plugins are disabled and why
2. Consider adding enable_plugin() and disable_plugin() methods to QBClient if programmatic control is needed in the future
3. Update CLAUDE.md to note that Jackett indexers are preferred over standalone tracker plugins
4. Document the current plugin configuration so it persists across qBittorrent reinstalls

**Implementation files:**
- patchy_bot/clients/qbittorrent.py — QBClient with list_search_plugins() at line 250
- patchy_bot/handlers/commands.py — health_report() uses plugin count at line 217
- CLAUDE.md or README.md — should document plugin strategy
tasks.testStrategy:
This is a documentation task for work already completed. Verification steps:
1. Run /health command and confirm it reports "7 enabled plugins" 
2. Run /plugins command and verify EZTV and YTS standalone plugins show as disabled (enabled=false)
3. Verify search results still include content from these trackers via Jackett indexers
4. Confirm the documentation accurately describes the plugin architecture and strategy
5. If QBClient methods are added, write unit tests mocking the /api/v2/search/enablePlugin endpoint

tasks.title: Document hash-based deduplication implementation
tasks.description: Document the completed work that added hash-based deduplication to the search result pipeline via deduplicate_results() in handlers/search.py, wired into bot.py's _run_search() and schedule search path to remove duplicate torrents while keeping the highest-seeded entry.
tasks.details:
This task documents work that was already completed as part of the search handler extraction and pipeline optimization. The deduplication system prevents multiple plugins from returning the same torrent by identifying duplicates via their info hash and consolidating them.

**What was implemented:**

1. **deduplicate_results() function** (handlers/search.py:108-139):
   - Takes a list of search result rows and removes duplicates that share the same 40-character hex info hash
   - When duplicates are found, keeps the entry with the highest seed count (more seeders = better availability)
   - Rows without a valid hash (non-hex or wrong length) are always preserved since they cannot be compared
   - Uses stable ordering: preserves the original position of first-seen entries in the output
   - Implementation: O(n) time complexity using a dictionary to track seen hashes by index

2. **Integration into search pipeline:**
   - bot.py _run_search() (line 2888): Applied after filtering, before sorting: `filtered → deduplicate_results() → sort_rows() → slice to limit`
   - bot.py schedule system (line 2449): Applied after filtering TV episode searches before episode matching
   - Both paths delegate through a static stub method _deduplicate_results() that calls search_handler.deduplicate_results()

3. **Pipeline order rationale:**
   - Deduplication happens AFTER filtering (apply_filters) to ensure quality/size/seed filters run first
   - Deduplication happens BEFORE sorting to reduce the number of rows being sorted
   - Deduplication happens BEFORE slicing to ensure the final page shows unique results

4. **Hash extraction logic:**
   - Checks both 'fileHash' and 'hash' keys to handle different plugin response formats
   - Validates hash format with regex: `[a-f0-9]{40}` (lowercase hex, exactly 40 chars)
   - Seed count extraction handles both 'nbSeeders' and 'seeders' keys (plugin format variance)

5. **Test coverage:**
   - tests/test_handlers.py has 2 tests for deduplicate_results() (lines 248-267):
     - test_deduplicate_keeps_best_seeder: Verifies highest-seeded entry is retained
     - test_deduplicate_keeps_no_hash_rows: Verifies rows without valid hashes are preserved

**Technical context:**
- This addresses the problem where multiple qBittorrent search plugins (Jackett, YTS, EZTV, etc.) often return the same torrent with different metadata
- Before deduplication: users saw the same movie/show 3-5 times in search results from different sources
- After deduplication: each unique torrent appears once, with the best-seeded source selected automatically
- Works alongside Task 14's plugin optimization (disabled duplicate standalone plugins EZTV/YTS)
tasks.testStrategy:
This is a documentation task for work already completed. Verification steps:

1. **Verify deduplication function exists and is wired:**
   - Confirm handlers/search.py contains deduplicate_results() function at line 108
   - Confirm bot.py line 2888 calls self._deduplicate_results(filtered) in _run_search()
   - Confirm bot.py line 2449 calls self._deduplicate_results(filtered) in schedule path
   - Confirm bot.py line 2753 has the delegation stub

2. **Run existing test suite:**
   - Execute: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_handlers.py::test_deduplicate_keeps_best_seeder -v`
   - Execute: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_handlers.py::test_deduplicate_keeps_no_hash_rows -v`
   - Both tests must pass

3. **Manual verification in production:**
   - Search for a popular movie (e.g., "Inception 2010") that will have many duplicate results across plugins
   - Verify search results show each unique torrent only once (no duplicate info hashes)
   - Verify that when duplicates exist, the entry with the most seeds is displayed
   - Check bot logs to confirm no errors during deduplication

4. **Code review confirmation:**
   - Read handlers/search.py lines 108-139 and verify the implementation matches the description
   - Verify the pipeline order: filter → deduplicate → sort → slice in bot.py _run_search()
   - Confirm the function handles missing/invalid hashes gracefully (no crashes on malformed data)

tasks.title: Document Jackett indexer expansion
tasks.description: Document the completed work that expanded Jackett from 6 to 9 indexers by researching 14 candidates, adding TorrentGalaxy, LimeTorrents, and TorrentDownloads, and removing KickassTorrents due to Cloudflare blocking. All new indexers were tested and verified to return results.
tasks.details:
This task documents work that was already completed outside of the tracked refactor tasks. The qBittorrent search system uses the Jackett plugin to aggregate multiple torrent indexers into a single unified search interface. Jackett acts as a proxy/translator between qBittorrent's search API and various torrent sites.

**What was implemented:**

1. **Research phase:** Evaluated 14 candidate indexers for addition to Jackett configuration
   - Assessed indexer reliability, content coverage, API availability, and Cloudflare status
   - Prioritized indexers with good TV and movie coverage that complement existing sources

2. **Added 3 new indexers:**
   - **TorrentGalaxy** — General-purpose indexer with strong TV and movie selection
   - **LimeTorrents** — Established indexer with diverse content catalog  
   - **TorrentDownloads** — Long-running tracker with broad torrent coverage
   - All three indexers were configured in Jackett's web UI and tested to confirm they return results

3. **Removed 1 broken indexer:**
   - **KickassTorrents** — Removed due to Cloudflare blocking preventing API access
   - Jackett could no longer reach this indexer, causing search timeouts and failures

4. **Net result:** Jackett indexer count increased from 6 to 9 active indexers
   - This expansion improves search result diversity and increases the likelihood of finding rare content
   - More indexers = better redundancy if individual trackers go offline

**How it integrates with the bot:**

The Jackett plugin is one of qBittorrent's search plugins (visible via `/plugins` command). When users search for media:
1. `QBClient.search()` (qbittorrent.py:59-152) calls qBittorrent's `/api/v2/search/start` endpoint with `plugin="enabled"`
2. qBittorrent queries all enabled plugins, including Jackett
3. Jackett fans out the query to all 9 configured indexers in parallel
4. Results from all indexers are aggregated and returned to the bot
5. `deduplicate_results()` (handlers/search.py:108-139) removes duplicate torrents that multiple indexers return
6. The `/health` command (handlers/commands.py:181-240) reports the count of enabled plugins via line 218

**No bot code changes were needed** because:
- Jackett configuration happens entirely in Jackett's web UI (external to the bot)
- qBittorrent's Jackett plugin automatically picks up the new indexers
- The bot's search pipeline already handles arbitrary plugin results
- Deduplication logic already removes cross-indexer duplicates

**Files involved (read-only reference):**
- `patchy_bot/clients/qbittorrent.py:59-152` — QBClient.search() orchestrates plugin searches
- `patchy_bot/handlers/search.py:108-139` — deduplicate_results() removes duplicate torrents
- `patchy_bot/handlers/commands.py:214-218` — /health reports enabled plugin count
- Jackett web UI (external) — where indexers were added/removed

**Configuration artifacts:**
- Jackett indexer configuration lives in Jackett's database (not in bot codebase)
- qBittorrent's plugin list accessible via `/api/v2/search/plugins` endpoint
- Bot observes these as read-only data sources
tasks.testStrategy:
This is a documentation task for work already completed. Verification steps:

1. **Verify indexer count:**
   - Run `/health` command in Telegram bot
   - Confirm it reports "qBittorrent search: ready (7 enabled plugins)" or similar
   - Note: The 7 plugins include Jackett (9 indexers) + other standalone plugins

2. **Verify Jackett indexers are active:**
   - Access Jackett web UI directly
   - Navigate to indexers list
   - Confirm TorrentGalaxy, LimeTorrents, and TorrentDownloads are configured and enabled
   - Confirm KickassTorrents is absent or disabled

3. **Test search functionality:**
   - Search for a popular movie via bot (e.g., "Inception 2010")
   - Verify results include torrents from multiple sources
   - Check result metadata in bot logs — should see varied `site` values indicating different indexers
   - Line 322-327 in handlers/search.py shows how site names are extracted from Jackett results

4. **Verify deduplication works:**
   - Search for a very popular torrent likely to appear on multiple indexers
   - Confirm bot doesn't show the same torrent multiple times
   - Check that deduplicate_results() in handlers/search.py:108-139 is being called in the search pipeline

5. **Check for Cloudflare errors:**
   - Review bot logs during searches
   - Confirm no Cloudflare timeout errors from Jackett
   - KickassTorrents removal should have eliminated these errors

6. **Plugin list verification:**
   - Run `/plugins` command in bot
   - Confirm Jackett plugin is listed as enabled
   - Verify the plugin count matches /health output

**No new automated tests needed** — this is documentation for infrastructure-level changes that don't affect bot code behavior. The existing search pipeline tests (task 12) already cover search result processing regardless of which indexers are configured.

tasks.title: Document skill-agent integration pilot implementation
tasks.description: Document the completed pilot work that added agent delegation sections to all 10 Patchy Bot project skills, created the plugin skill routing reference at ~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md mapping 27 plugin skills, and completed Phase 2 agent mapping analysis for all 131 skills.
tasks.details:
This task documents work already completed as part of the skill-agent integration pilot. The work systematically embedded agent delegation guidance into Patchy Bot's custom skills and created a comprehensive routing reference for plugin skills.

**What was implemented:**

1. **Project skill embedding (10 skills):**
   - Added "Agent Delegation" sections to all 10 Patchy Bot custom skills in `.claude-plugin/skills/*/SKILL.md`
   - Skills updated: check-logs, db-inspect, debug-schedule, env-check, gh-issues-auto-fixer, restart, sync-parity, telegram-chat-polisher, telegram-ux-architect, test-bot
   - Each section specifies:
     - Primary agent (which project/general agent handles the implementation)
     - Review agent (which agent performs post-implementation review, typically security-auditor)
     - Dispatch mode (sequential, parallel, standalone, or "No delegation" for process/meta skills)
   - Format follows: "Delegate <action> to the `agent-name` (sequential with review)" pattern
   - Example from check-logs/SKILL.md:12-17: "Delegate log retrieval and service status checks to the `config-infra-agent` (sequential with review). If logs reveal errors or crashes, delegate root cause diagnosis to the `error-detective` agent."

2. **Plugin skill routing reference (27 plugin skills):**
   - Created `~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md` (253 lines)
   - Documents agent mappings for 27 plugin skills across 12 plugins:
     - superpowers plugin (16 skills) — mixed delegation patterns
     - pai plugin (6 skills) — mostly no delegation (uses own researcher agents)
     - claude-md-management plugin (2 skills) — config management, no delegation
     - code-review plugin (1 skill) — delegates to security-auditor
     - feature-dev plugin (1 skill) — delegates to python-pro + security-auditor
     - agent-sdk-dev plugin (1 skill) — delegates to python-pro
     - plugin-dev plugin (7 skills) — meta skills, no delegation
     - hookify plugin (4 skills) — process/config skills, no delegation
     - semgrep-plugin (2 skills) — setup skills, no delegation
     - chrome-devtools-mcp plugin (4 skills) — mixed (uses MCP tools + performance-engineer/error-detective)
     - microsoft-docs plugin (3 skills) — reference/meta skills, no delegation
     - mcp-server-dev plugin (3 skills) — delegates to python-pro
     - claude-code-setup plugin (1 skill) — analysis skill, no delegation
     - custom-skills (7 skills) — includes telegram-developer→ui-agent, database-expert→database-agent, torrent-search→search-download-agent
   - Each entry includes: skill name, primary agent, review agent, dispatch mode
   - File also includes future Phase 4 mapping for ~94 user-level skills (process, language/framework, infrastructure, security, data/API, ML/AI, other)

3. **Phase 2 mapping analysis (131 skills total):**
   - Analyzed all skills for agent routing requirements
   - Breakdown: 94 user-level skills + 10 project skills + 27 plugin skills = 131 total
   - Confirmed that existing 21 general agents (python-pro, fastapi-developer, cli-developer, docker-expert, quant-analyst, security-engineer, security-auditor, penetration-tester, error-detective, performance-engineer, incident-responder, dependency-manager, etc.) cover all skill domains
   - No gaps found — no new agents need to be created
   - User-level skill embedding deferred to follow-up sessions (Phase 4)

4. **CLAUDE.md routing rule:**
   - The user description mentions "added CLAUDE.md routing rule" but this appears to be pending or not yet committed
   - Expected location: ~/Patchy_Bot/CLAUDE.md after the "Subagent Routing" section
   - Expected content: A new section instructing Claude to consult `~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md` when activating plugin skills

**Impact:**

- All 10 Patchy Bot project skills now have explicit agent delegation guidance
- Plugin skill routing is centralized and documented for 27 skills across 12 plugins
- Claude will automatically delegate to the correct agents when skills are activated
- Reduces inline implementation in main session — enforces subagent-driven development
- Phase 2 complete; Phase 4 (user-level skill embedding) is the next step

**File locations:**

- Project skills: `/home/karson/Patchy_Bot/.claude-plugin/skills/*/SKILL.md` (10 files)
- Plugin routing reference: `~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md` (1 file, 253 lines)
- CLAUDE.md routing rule: Expected in `/home/karson/Patchy_Bot/CLAUDE.md` (status: pending verification)
tasks.testStrategy:
This is a documentation task for work already completed. Verification steps:

1. **Verify project skill embedding (10 skills):**
   - Run: `grep -l "Agent Delegation" /home/karson/Patchy_Bot/.claude-plugin/skills/*/SKILL.md | wc -l`
   - Expected output: `10` (confirms all 10 skills have the section)
   - Spot-check 3 random skills to verify format matches expected pattern with Primary/Review/Dispatch

2. **Verify plugin routing reference exists:**
   - Run: `ls -lh ~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md`
   - Expected: File exists, ~253 lines, ~10KB size
   - Run: `grep -c "^| " ~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md`
   - Expected: Significant number of table rows (mapping entries)

3. **Verify plugin skill count:**
   - Count skills documented in routing reference: `grep -E "^### |^## " ~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md`
   - Expected: 12 plugin sections + user-level skills section

4. **Verify CLAUDE.md routing rule:**
   - Run: `grep -i "plugin.*skill.*routing" /home/karson/Patchy_Bot/CLAUDE.md`
   - If no match: routing rule is pending and should be added
   - Expected location: After "Subagent Routing" section (around line 115)
   - Expected content: "When activating any plugin skill, consult `~/.claude/references/plugin-skill-agent-routing.md` for the recommended agent delegation pattern."

5. **Functional test:**
   - Activate a plugin skill (e.g., /telegram-developer)
   - Verify Claude consults the routing reference
   - Verify Claude delegates to ui-agent as specified in the routing table

6. **Gap analysis verification:**
   - Confirm no missing agents were identified during Phase 2 analysis
   - All 131 skills map to existing 21 general agents + 9 project agents
